coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode fluorimetric and colorimetric sensors based on iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for the detection of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Peng
Shang, Mingzhao
Xie, Ruyan
Gao, Yu
Tian, Miaomiao
Dai, Qijun
Zhang, Fang
Chai, Fang
coredata.dc:description: Determination of dopamine (DA) is crucial for its intimate relationship with clinical trials and biological environment. Herein, Fe, N co-doped carbon dots (AFC-CDs) were fabricated by optimizing precursors and reaction conditions for fluorimetric/colorimetric dual-mode sensing of DA. With synergistic influence of Förster resonance energy transfer and static quenching effect, DA significantly quenched the blue luminescence of AFC-CDs at 442 nm, the production of recognizable tan-brown complex caused evident colorimetric response, achieved the dual-mode fluorimetric/colorimetric sensing for DA. The excellent selectivity and satisfied sensitivity can be confirmed with the limit of detection at 0.29 μM and 2.31 μM via fluorimetric/colorimetric mode respectively. The reliability and practicability were proved by recovery of 94.81–101.61% in real samples. Notably, the proposed electron transfer way between AFC-CDs and DA was hypothesized logically, indicated dual-mode probe provided a promising platform for the sensing of trace DA, and could be expanded in environment and food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Detection
Dopamine
Fluorimetric and colorimetric
Dual-mode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of DA in actual samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22171060), Purple Mountain Talent Jiangning Baijiahu project-major special project of linkedin project (2022D027).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of AFC-CDs (Inset: HRTEM image). (b) FT-IR spectra of AFC, DETA and AFC-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C 1 s, (d) N 1 s, (e) O 1 s and (f) Fe 2p. (g) UV–vis absorption and FL spectra of AFC-CDs (Inset: corresponding images under daylight and 365 nm UV light). (h) The 3D contour of AFC-CDs. (i) Emission of the AFC-CDs at various excitation wavelengths (340–390 nm) (Inset: CIE 1931 (x, y) chromaticity diagram of AFC-CDs).

Fluorimetric detection (AFC-CDs were diluted to 2000 times as FL probe, and incubation time was 40 min): The FL spectra of AFC-CDs probe with various metal ions (200 μM) (a) and biomolecules (200 μM) (b) The corresponding I/I0 versus various metal ions (200 μM, blue) and (c) biomolecules (200 μM, green), (d) after adding DA (200 μM, pink). (e) FL spectra of AFC-CDs exposed to a series of concentration gradients DA (0–200 μM). (f) The linear relationship between I0/I and DA concentration (inset: Corresponding photograph under UV light). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Colorimetric detection (AFC-CDs were diluted to 2000 times as FL probe, and incubation time was 40 min): (a) The UV–vis absorption of AFC-CDs with various metal ions (200 μM). (b) The UV–vis absorption of AFC-CDs with various biomolecules (200 μM). (c) The absorption of AFC-CDs with a serial concentration gradients DA (0–500 μM). (d) The linear correlation of ΔA with concentration of DA (Illustration: Corresponding colorimetric image).

Exploration of mechanism: (a) The UV–vis absorption of AFC-CDs (green), DA (red) and AFC-CDs + DA (blue), dotted: the physical summation of AFC-CDs and DA spectra (blank). (b) The absorption of DA (green) and AFC-CDs + DA (purple), and emission of AFC-CDs (red) excited at 365 nm. (c) FL lifetime of AFC-CDs and AFC-CDs + DA. (4) The Zeta potentials of AFC-CDs, DA and AFC-CDs + DA. (e) The presumptive mechanism of AFC-CDs sensing DA. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Graphic illustration of the synthesis and optimization (A) and the utilization (B) of AFC-CDs.
Sample detection results of our dual-mode FL/colorimetric method.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode fluorimetric and colorimetric sensors based on iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for the detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Heilongjiang Province, Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin 150025, Heilongjiang Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials
Heilongjiang Province, Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Heilongjiang Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Purple Mountain Laboratories, Mozhou East Road, Nanjing, Jiangsu Province, 211111, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Purple Mountain Laboratories, Mozhou East Road, Nanjing, Jiangsu Province, 211111, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Determination of dopamine (DA) is crucial for its intimate relationship with clinical trials and biological environment. Herein, Fe, N co-doped carbon dots (AFC-CDs) were fabricated by optimizing precursors and reaction conditions for fluorimetric/colorimetric dual-mode sensing of DA. With synergistic influence of Förster resonance energy transfer and static quenching effect, DA significantly quenched the blue luminescence of AFC-CDs at 442 nm, the production of recognizable tan-brown complex caused evident colorimetric response, achieved the dual-mode fluorimetric/colorimetric sensing for DA. The excellent selectivity and satisfied sensitivity can be confirmed with the limit of detection at 0.29 μM and 2.31 μM via fluorimetric/colorimetric mode respectively. The reliability and practicability were proved by recovery of 94.81–101.61% in real samples. Notably, the proposed electron transfer way between AFC-CDs and DA was hypothesized logically, indicated dual-mode probe provided a promising platform for the sensing of trace DA, and could be expanded in environment and food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine, as a sort of essential neurotransmitter in catecholamines, plays a vital role in renal hormones, cardiovascular system and central nervous system, which regulate and influence people's physical, mental and emotional experiences, and almost all functions in the body are controlled by neurotransmitters (Berke, 2018). Numerous studies have proven that abnormal DA levels can lead to diseases such as Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, depression and dementia (Schultz, 2007). Notably, DA can accelerate the decomposition of animal body fat and protein deposition, which can improve the lean meat rate and feed conversion rate of livestock. Thus, it is illegally used as “lean meat essence” to feed livestock in husbandry production to urge meat can be put on the public for human consumption in advance, though it is prohibited internationally (Li et al., 2020). However, long-term intake of food with residual DA will lead to palpitation, headache, metabolic disorder and other adverse reactions (Mo et al., 2001). To combat these illegal actions, assure the food safety, it is of great meaningful to explore a reliable method for detection of DA.

So far, there are numerous analytical techniques for DA like biomolecules (Yang et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023), such as surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (Figueiredo et al., 2020), capillary electrophoresis, high performance liquid chromatography and cyclic voltammetry (Rana et al., 2023). Although these methods can supply satisfactory selectivity and lower limit of detection (LOD), their shortage in complicated sample preparation procedures, expensive instruments, long analysis time and lack of skilled training of operators restrict their extensive application severely. By contrast, fluorescent (FL) sensors, such as metal nanoclusters (Ling et al., 2020), metal–organic frameworks and carbon nanomaterials (Ghosh et al., 2022), are extensively applied to the quantitative analysis of DA because of their outstanding sensitivity and rapid response.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel carbon nano-material, have attracted much attention owing to their superior optical properties, good chemical stability, unique biocompatibility, easy preparation and low budget. CDs are also quite sensitive to charges transfer, which can modify their optical properties and arouse people's curiosity in charge-transfer-based sensing (Huang et al., 2023). To ameliorate the chemiluminescence characteristics of CDs, doping heteroatoms such as nonmetallic atoms and metal ions into CDs has been reported as a feasible method and achieve excellent fabrication and construction of CDs, because heteroatom doping can effectively modulate the band gap width and electronic structure of CDs (Miao et al., 2020). Thus the heteroatom doping improved the optical properties, further prompted the potential utility in sensing, imaging etc.. Compared with most nonmetallic heteroatoms, metal ions have more unoccupied electron orbitals and are assigned larger atomic size than nonmetallic atoms, which will govern the biochemical properties of CDs to a greater extent (Li et al., 2021). To date, a considerable number of heteroatom-doped CDs have been reported. For instance, Ren et al. prepared CDs by hydrothermal method with citric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials could achieve a LOD as low as 1 nM for DA analysis (Ren et al., 2016). Tiwari et al. obtained CDs and N-CDs through one-step hydrothermal synthesis as FL probes for the detection of DA in human serum with LOD of 54 μM and 5.12 μM respectively (Tiwari et al., 2022). Though many outstanding detection work for DA have be developed, the complicated redox characteristics and electron transfer laws of DA reflected by its unique structure on benzene ring are still lacking in a systematic understanding regrettably.

Otherwise, a crucial issue of the probes merely offer single-signal FL response should be notice, for their application circumstances of these single-signal tests could be seriously limited (e.g., dark samples can't be accurately quantified by naked-eye or colorimetry). In comparison, dual-mode detection have demonstrated attractive advantages such as high anti-interference ability, and excellent visualization and satisfied sensitivity (Zhou et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2024). In one aspect, dual-mode probe supplies selectable signals to cater for the requirement of perceiving targets in complex scenarios (Xu et al., 2022). In another aspect, the data acquired can be mutually verified, which achieve self-calibration of detected results and obtain a more reliable method (Ajith et al., 2022). Considering this, a kind of dual-mode fluorimetric/colorimetric analytical probe iron and nitrogen co-doped CDs (AFC-CDs) was designed and fabricated. As described in Scheme 1 , AFC-CDs with superior optical properties and abundant superficial functional groups were synthesized by a simple one-step solvothermal method. When DA was introduced, both colorimetric and fluorimetric response occurred with a visible colorimetric evolution from colorless to brown and FL quenching evidently, which resulted from the synergistic impact of static quenching effect (SQE) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). The dual-mode fluorimetric/colorimetric probe for DA achieved the real sensing in human and mouse serum with high sensibility, selectivity and feasibility. Profoundly, a very plausible electron transfer way between AFC-CDs and DA was hypothesized logically, which also contributed a valuable reference for the future work in DA sensing.

All the reagents mentioned in the experiment were employed directly after being purchased, without any other special treatment. Diethylenetriamine (DETA), serine (Ser) and citrulline (Citn) were sourced from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Iron ammonium citrate (AFC) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (China). Tryptophan (Trp) was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Institute of Fine Chemical Industry (China). Dopamine (DA), cysteine (Cys), phenylalanine (Phe), bovine serum albumin (BSA), methionine (Met), glutamic acid (Glu), glutathione (GSH), ascorbic acid (AA), lysine (Lys), leucine (Leu), urea (Ur), uric acid (UA), glucose (Glc), threonine (Thr), aspartic acid (Asp) and histidine (His) were all bought from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). Lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), cadmium nitrate (Cd(NO3)·4H2O), silver nitrate (AgNO3), chromium nitrate (Cr(NO3)3·9H2O), zinc acetate (Zn(AC)2·2H2O), manganese acetate (Mn(AC)2·4H2O), calcium chloride (CaCl2·2H2O), ferrous sulfate (FeSO4·7H2O), cobalt sulfate (CoSO4·7H2O), mercury nitrate (Hg(NO3)2·2H2O), magnesium nitrate (Mg(NO3)2·6H2O) and nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (China). Ferric sulfate (Fe2(SO4)3) was obtained from Tianjin Tianda Chemical Reagent Factory (China). Deionized water (resistivity ≥ 18.2 MΩ·cm) prepared by ultrapure water system was adopted in all experimental steps.

Several measurement and characterization means were applied to analyze the morphology, properties and detection performance of AFC-CDs. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were taken by Tecnai F20 (USA) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was observed using Philips X'Pert X-ray diffractometer (Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were analyzed on JASCOFT/IR-420 infrared spectrometer (Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were acquired with Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (USA). The FL spectra were gained by LENGGUANG F98 (China) and HORIBA Jobin Yvon FL-1000 (France). The FL analysis images of AFC-CDs irradiated at 365 nm were obtained utilizing WFH-204B portable UV light (China). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded by UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, Japan). Zeta potential were carried out by Nanobrook-Zeta PALS potential analyzer (USA).

The AFC-CDs were prepared according to a simple solvothermal process analogous to that reported in the published literature. Concretely, AFC and DETA were adopted as iron source and carbon source respectively, and the nitrogen can be offered collectively. As illustrated in Scheme 1A, 0.41 mM of AFC was dissolved in 10 mL DETA and stirred for 10 min. Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at constant temperature. Various optical properties of AFC-CDs could be synthesized via adjusting different reaction temperature, reaction time and precursor molar ratio. The fluorescence intensity of AFC-CDs was compared to obtain the optimal synthesis condition by measuring emission, taking photos and grabbing colors, so as to determine the best sample for sensing. Besides, as a control, the CDs sample were synthesized by the same procedure with AFC or DETA solely as reference. In comparison, the AFC-CDs exhibited strongest FL intensity than the samples prepared by AFC and DETA solely, indicated the co-doped iron and nitrogen endowed AFC-CDs superior surface functional groups, and resulted in the advantageous optical property (Fig. S1 a). The continuous exploration demonstrated that AFC-CDs obtained at 6 h and 200 °C was the best sample (Fig. S1b, c). With the rising of the molar ratio (AFC: DETA), the FL intensity tended to be increasingly, and the molar ratio of 1: 219 was chosen as the ultimate condition (Fig. S1d). The regulation of synthesis of AFC-CDs was carried out and achieved an effective probe. The final product was dark-brown turbid liquid, appeared extremely bright blue luminescence (λem = 365 nm) under UV light. The result product was naturally cooled to ambient temperature, and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min. The collected supernatant was further purified by filtration (0.22 μM filter membrane) and dialysis (MWCO: 1000 Da, 24 h) to remove impurities. The obtained AFC-CDs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further application. The optimum AFC-CDs synthesized during the reaction at 180 °C for 8 h with the molar ratio of AFC to DETA at 1: 219 (Fig. S2) was eventually selected as the colorimetric analysis probe.

To evaluate the selectivity and possible interference, 15 relative metal ion solutions (Ca2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+ and Mg2+) and 19 typical bio-molecule solutions (Ser, Citn, Trp, DA, Cys, Phe, BSA, Met, Glu, GSH, AA, Lys, Leu, Ur, UA, Glc, Thr, Asp, His) were investigated. The AFC-CDs stock solution was diluted by 2000 times with ethanol and employed as probe, for sensing by mixing equivalent volume of samples. Then the fluorimetric/colorimetric response was analyzed by measuring fluorescence and absorbance after incubation for 35–40 min. The anti-interference performance of AFC-CDs was evaluated by establishing the ratio (AFC-CDs/DA/interfering substance) as 1: 1: 1 and measuring the fluorescence and absorbance. To further explore the sensitivity, different concentrations of DA (0–200 μM) with AFC-CDs were evaluated the performance of sensing DA at same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Blood samples were collected from healthy adult male volunteers and laboratory mice, respectively, and treated according to the guidelines for the nursing care and utilization of laboratory animals issued by the National Institutes of Health (Albus, 2012). Human serum was obtained from the volunteers with a clear source and reasonable access. All procedures were performed in compliance with relevant laws and institutional guidelines and obtained the approval of appropriate institutional committees. The mice were not euthanized either during or after this procedure. Pork was purchased from local supermarket, and water collected from Songhua River, the above samples were selected as authentic samples to verify the practicability of the AFC-CDs probe. Mouse blood and human blood were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 20 min, and the supernatant was gathered as serum samples, after diluted 500 times by deionized water, the serum samples were prepared for further use. The pork sample was pretreatment according to the reported approach with minor adjustment (Li et al., 2021). All samples were filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove insoluble impurities. All real samples were first tested by AFC-CDs probe to determine the existence of DA, then the standard addition method was adopted to evaluate the recovery. The spiked samples were prepared by mixing different concentrations of DA with the sample solution.

The morphology and size of AFC-CDs were initially characterized by TEM (Fig. 1a), the as-prepared AFC-CDs possessed good dispersibility and almost homogeneous size with average diameter of 2.0 nm within particle scale of 0.8–4.0 nm. The high crystallinity of AFC-CDs was embodied perfectly in high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images, the lattice spacings of 0.18 nm and 0.34 nm were allocated to the (100) and (002) crystal planes of graphite carbon respectively (Zhou et al., 2022), and the lattice spacing of 0.25 nm can be ascribed to the (311) crystal plane of Fe3O4 (inset of Fig. 1a) (Song et al., 2023), confirmed the doping of Fe element. By analyzing the morphology of AFC-CDs, the Fe2+/Fe3+ initially combined with functional groups in solution through intermolecular coordination during the synthesis process, which resulted in the formation of macromolecular structures with numerous groups. Then interacted with the DETA solvent and transformed into AFC-CDs with a large conjugated sp2 architecture and enriched by surface groups. The tight interaction between AFC-CDs and high crystalline Fe3O4 prevented the agglomeration of nanoscale Fe3O4, which was favorable for its fluorescence stability. The XRD diffraction of AFC-CDs appeared a large peak at 2θ = 24 (Fig. S3), corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphite, which verified the sp2 hybrid carbon-core structure of AFC-CDs (Geng et al., 2021). Different from the reported Fe-doped CDs (Song et al., 2023), the characteristic diffraction pattern of Fe3O4 was not found, and correspondingly, the magnetism was also absent, indicating that even if all the doping of Fe was present in an oxidized state, the amount was very small.

To acquire a profound insight into the elemental composition and functional groups of AFC-CDs, FTIR (Fig. 1b) and XPS (Fig. 1c-f) of AFC-CDs and precursors was characterized and illustrated. The characteristic peaks at ∼ 3430 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of −NH2, illustrating that AFC-CDs contained a certain number of amino groups (Meng et al., 2021). The CH stretch (∼2920 cm−1) (Ran et al., 2022), CO/CC stretch (∼1633 cm−1) (Chen et al., 2022), CN stretch (∼1563 cm−1) (Cao et al., 2022), CN stretch (∼1334/817 cm−1) and NH bend (∼1484 cm−1) can be distinguished in the AFC-CDs sample clearly (Fig. 1b, red) (Tao et al., 2022). Also, the weak broad peak in the range of 600–660 cm−1 may be associated with the tensile vibration of N-Fe-N or Fe-O (Li et al., 2022), further confirming the fact of Fe doping. Compared with data of AFC and DETA, similar peaks can be noticed, demonstrated AFC-CDs inherited sectional natural attributes of the precursors, which provided abundant surficial groups and active site benefits for coordination. The XPS full-scan spectrum (Fig. S4) exhibited the predominant peaks of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Fe 2p located at 284.8 eV, 400.2 eV, 532.3 eV and 711.1 eV, with their atomic contents of C (75.01%, Fig. 1c), N (6.16%, Fig. 1d), O (17.59%, Fig. 1e) and Fe (1.24%, Fig. 1f), the little Fe content echoed the XRD results. The high-resolution of C 1s exhibited three main peaks centered at 284.8, 286.8 and 288.8 eV, which were respectively attributed to CC, CO/CN, and CO bonds (Fig. 1c) (He et al., 2018). The N 1s displayed two different categories of N: pyridinic type N (400.1 eV) and graphitic type N (401.9 eV) (Park et al., 2021), the N doping CDs faciliated the enhancement of blue fluorescence was approved widely (Li et al, 2019; Dhenadhayalan et al, 2020). The O 1s was deconvolved into three characteristic peaks, attributed to FeOH (531.4 eV), COC (532.6 eV) and CO (534.1 eV) (Luo et al., 2022). The Fe 2p3/2 (711.2 eV), Fe 2p1/2 (724.6 eV), and corresponding two strong shake-up satellites at 715 eV and 728 eV can be found, indicating the Fe presented in AFC-CDs as α-Fe2O3 form (Wang et al., 2021; Ibraheem et al, 2023). The results illustrated the surface of AFC-CDs distributed abundant –COOH and –NH2 functional groups, which facilitated to providing the active sites for coordination with DA.

The optical feature of AFC-CDs was expounded in detail by UV–vis absorption and FL spectra. The UV–vis absorption of AFC-CDs appeared a recognizable shoulder peak at 290 nm (Fig. 1g), assigned to the aromatic n-π* transition of CO (Wei et al., 2022). AFC-CDs probe appeared transparent colorless solution and blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 1g inset), with optimal emission of AFC-CDs at 442 nm under the optimal excitation (365 nm), which can also be intuitively acquired from 3D contour (Fig. 1h), indicating the high-purity blue fluorescence of AFC-CDs. The excitation-dependent feature was proved by a slight red shift of emission from 443 to 448 nm with the excitation wavelength 340 to 390 nm (Fig. 1i), manifested the discrepancies of surface size distribution and chemical properties (Ghosh et al., 2022; Sharma et al., 2016). The CIE can be found focusing in a concentrated blue range (Fig. 2 d), illustrated the slight evolution of emission under different citations with negligible influence. Subsequently, the fluorescence quantumyield (QY) of AFC-CDs was calculated to be 23.89% by using quinine sulfate as reference (Supplementary Materials) (Kaur et al., 2023).

Considering the FL stability of the probe is crucial to its subsequent applications, the effects of pH, ionic strength, ultraviolet irradiation duration, and long-period storage on the FL intensity of AFC-CDs were estimated serially. The as-prepared AFC-CDs exhibited relatively steady FL intensity in PBS buffer solution with a pH range of 2–12 (Fig. S5a), the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I/I0) exhibited over 90% at a large range of pH (3–12), indicating the excellent FL stability of AFC-CDs at various pHs. The FL intensity presented almost no change in salt solution with NaCl concentration ranging from 0.2 to 1 M (Fig. S5b), continuous ultraviolet irradiation (365 nm) for 6 h (Fig. S5c), and long-period storage for 50 days (Fig. S5d), which suggested the superior optical robustness of AFC-CDs, with the outstanding FL stability and environmental tolerance, which benefited the sensing performance and facilitated practical utility. To optimize the sensing behavior of AFC-CDs toward DA, the response of FL intensity via various incubation times was also explored. The FL response can be recognized with the incubation time at 35 min (Fig. S6), and the absorbance occurred significant enhancement and remained stability after incubating for 40 min (Fig. S7), thus, 35/40 min was considered as the appropriate fluorimetric/colorimetric response time in the subsequent analysis.

The sensing performance was investigated under optimized conditions. The as-synthesized AFC-CDs were diluted 2000 times and then employed as FL probe in this work. A certain concentration of DA caused the color transformation and quenched the fluorescence of the AFC-CDs probe (Scheme 1B), accompanied by the signal attenuation of FL and an increase in the UV–vis spectra, respectively. The selectivity toward DA was estimated by testing the FL spectra of AFC-CDs with 200 μM of metal ions and related biomolecules (Fig. 2a, b). The FL intensity with the other samples, regardless of metals or biomolecules, demonstrated a neglected change in the FL intensity of AFC-CDs. By contrast, the FL intensity of the probe with DA plunged distinctly can be observed, with a plummeted QY of 5.37% from 23.89%, signified the excellent specificity for sensing DA. The column charts of the relative intensity ratio (I0/I) (I0 and I were denoted as the emission intensity of AFC-CDs without and with interference components, respectively) were employed to present intuitive data, manifested decent selectivity of detection (Fig. S8a, b), which was also verified by the corresponding photographs under UV light (Fig. S9a, b). Subsequently, the interference of detection was also estimated to assure their possibility of real application. The FL intensity of AFC-CDs (Fig. 2c, d) attenuated dramatically, implied that the coexistence of interfering substances could not disturb the monitoring of DA, and confirmed that the interference immunity of the probe was beyond suspicion. The sensitivity of detection was further assessed by testing the FL intensity with various concentrations of DA (Fig. 2e). When the concentration of DA was gradually increased from 0.1 to 500 μM, the FL intensity of AFC-CDs slumped regularly, which matched the rhythmical changes of FL intensity under UV light, illustrated the dependence of DA concentration on FL intensity. The FL quenching presented a linearity accompanied with the rising concentration of DA in the range of 0.3–7 μM (Fig. 2f), and the correlative equation was fitted depending on deduction as I0/I = 0.0056x + 1.0087 (R2 = 0.992, I0 = blank and I = AFC-CDs + DA), with the LOD of 0.29 μM based on 3σ/k (n = 11), (σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution, and k is the slope of the calibration profile) (Fan et al., 2024). The delightful sensing method provided a substantial guarantee for the feasibility of detecting DA based on the evolution of the FL intensity of AFC-CDs, indicating the effectiveness of the fluorimetric method for DA.

In the process of DA analysis, with the FL of AFC-CDs quenched gradually, the color evolution of the solution deepened slowly in sunlight, which enlightened us to further ponder the feasibility of colorimetric analysis. AFC-CDs substantially amplified the absorbance signal of DA at 278 nm (Fig. S10). The observable magnification of the signal portended that the probe applied to the colorimetric analysis of DA would achieve a wider detection range with a lower LOD. To ensure the specificity of AFC-CDs for signal augmentation of target analytes, the UV–vis spectra of common metal ions and biomolecules participated in the comparison (Fig. 3 a, b). Other sample except DA cannot evoke colorimetric enhancement at 278 nm, which harmoniously corresponded to the phenomenon witnessed in daylight (Fig. S11a, b). The outstanding selectivity confirmed with the reliable recognization for DA. Under the optimum response time, with the raising of DA concentration level, the absorbance at 278 nm exhibited regular enhancement (Fig. 3c), with corresponding colorimetric evolution from shallow to deep gradually. Synchronously, the absorbance variation values ΔA (A-A0) displayed a satisfactory linear relationship (y = 0.0034x + 0.076, R2 = 0.996) with DA concentration at range of 0.3–100 μM with a low relative standard deviation (Fig. 3d). The LOD of 2.31 μM was determined based on 3σ/k. The comparison between our presented method and the reported DA sensing strategies was arranged in Table S1, which adequately certified AFC-CDs comparable or even preferable to other reported FL probes. Notably, the dual-mode probe with a dual-signal output obtained reliable and robust performance, avoid unreliable data caused by the single optical detection method. These coincident results manifested the dual-mode AFC-CDs probe possessed the sensing capability for DA with excellent accuracy and feasibility simultaneously. And the dual-signal based fluorimetric/colorimetric strategy presented reliable analysis of DA, which can be employed to detect DA in complex circumstances with reliability and validity.

Given the excellent performance of AFC-CDs in sensing DA via fluorimetric and colorimetric dual-mode, it was fascinating to conduct a comprehensive exploration of its plausible sensing mechanism. Compared with the theoretical curve (Fig. 4 a), the absorption peak of the mixture of AFC-CDs and DA appeared a tiny red shift, and accompanied by the broadening of the peak shape, their superposed area with the excitation peak of AFC-CDs far exceeded the overlapped region of the excitation peak and the absorption of DA (Fig. 4b), manifesting the chemical interaction between AFC-CDs and DA and generating an unsuspected compound (Dai et al, 2022). The oxidation of DA to produce DA-quinone (DQ) species by surrounding oxygen has been reported. The potent electron capture ability of DQ could be used as an electron acceptor to trap the photogenerated electrons of CDs and quench their fluorescence (Wang et al., 2020). Due to possessing abundant amino groups of AFC-CDs, the alkalescent reaction environment was provided and facilitated the oxidation of DA to create a predominant condition for the generation of DQ (Medintz et al., 2010). The emergence of new substances universally resulted in static quenching effect (SQE) (Zhang et al., 2021), which illustrated the mechanism of FL quenching and the colorimetric response.

Moreover, the large-area spectra superposition also implied the probability of an internal filtration effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the unknown compound and AFC-CDs (Tang et al., 2023). To validate this assumption, the corresponding FL lifetime was also determined. The FL decay curves of AFC-CDs before and after treatment with DA were fitted by the listed double exponential function:
(1)
I
t
=
A 1
e x p
- t /
τ 1
+
A 2
e x p
- t /
τ 2
Herein, τ1 and τ2 are stipulated as the decay time constants of different radioactive decay channels, and A1 and A2 are designated as the correspondent amplitudes. On the basis of the data provided by the fitting results, the average FL lifetime was acquired by adhering to the following formula:
(2)
τ
average
=
A 1
×
τ
1
2
+
A 2
×
τ
2
2
/
A 1
×
τ 1
+
A 2
×
τ 2
The FL lifetime of AFC-CDs underwent severe decay with the addition of DA, from 9.02 μs to 6.92 μs (Fig. 4c), which hinted that the FRET triggered FL quenching rather than IFE, because the FL lifetime of the donor remained stationary in IFE but dropped evidently during FRET (Zhang et al., 2018). The TEM analysis of AFC-CDs introduced DA was also performed to illustrate and confirm the deduction (Fig. S12). The apparent massive agglomeration portended the existence of electrostatic attraction between AFC-CDs and DA, which induced the complexing effect visible to the naked eye. Then the zeta potentials of AFC-CDs and DA (Fig. 4d) were evaluated to further determine the deduction of FRET, the opposite potentials of −11.72 mV (AFC-CDs) and +3.20 mV (DA) testified the occurrence of electrostatic attraction, which enabled the donor–acceptor distance to be smaller (<10 nm) and met our expectations totally.

According to the foregoing exploration, a probable electron transfer pattern in the reaction process was inferenced (Fig. 4e). With a moderate photon energy (hv), the electrons of AFC-CDs were energized from valence band (VB) towards conduction band (CB), which were prone to combine with protons on DA (Medintz et al., 2010). The enormous hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of AFC-CDs revealed by FTIR and XPS displayed phenol-like characteristics and appeared weakly acidic for the resonant structure of the ionizable aromatic part in the graphite cores, which enabled it to accommodate the release of electrons from the basic amine group of DA and form alkaline polyphenolic ions. Then DA exhibited better ionization (pKa≈ 9) and redox characteristics after losing amino electrons owing to the existence of two acidic protons from the phenolic hydroxyl group attached to the benzene ring (Armstrong et al., 1976), and the electrons adhered to the polyphenol ion of AFC-CDs were recaptured via the phenolic hydroxyl terminal. Finally, the acidic protons of DA were extracted by anions on the surface of AFC-CDs, and the negative charges of oxygen atoms delocalized through resonant structures of benzene rings to form DQ, which triggered fluorescence quenching of AFC-CDs with colorimetric evolution (Chellasamy et al., 2021). Overall, in the process of sensing DA with AFC-CDs, the FL was quenched appreciably based on the synergism of SQE and FRET, and a dark-colored complex suspected to be DQ was generated for electrostatic attraction and electron transfer between AFC-CDs CB and DA.

The practical adaptability of AFC-CDs was evaluated by monitoring the commercial pork, human serum, mouse serum and Songhua River water as real samples. Because sensing DA in serum can provide pivotal data for evaluating the activity of the hormonal, cardiovascular, renal, and central nervous system. And the analysis of DA in pork can monitor the illegal food additives effectively. As summarized in Table 1 , both the colorimetric and fluorimetric results were almost consistent with the spiked values. The recoveries of 93.1–104.9% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 5% were obtained (Table 1). The results manifested the proposed probe which presented even more advantage in the reliability and feasibility, indicated the developed dual-mode analytical method would be extensively applied to the analysis of DA in complex matrices.

In summary, the dual-mode fluorimetric/colorimetric probe Fe, N co-doped AFC-CDs was designed and fabricated by modulating the precursors ratio and reaction conditions. The FL of AFC-CDs was remarkably quenched by DA based on SQE and FRET mechanisms, with the generation of a dark-brown complex because of electrostatic attraction. Benefiting from the specific complexation reaction and dual-channel signal output, the magnifying analysis of DA with a broad range and a low LOD was determined, and the LOD acquired by FL and colorimetric sensing were 0.29 and 2.31 μM, respectively. Notably, this method equally reflected excellent feasibility and robustness in the practical sample analysis, and the deduced recovery ranged from 93.1 to 104.9% with RSD limited to below 5%. This work not only presented a novel bifunctional sensing strategy based on AFC-CDs to assay DA, but also featured with low cost, easy design and simple operation, which will prompt more attempts to explore potential heteroatom-doped CDs and their applications in the field of biochemical sensing, and provided a promising perspective for environmental analysis and food safety.

Ethical Statement: Human serum was obtained from the volunteers with clear source and reasonable access. All procedures were performed in compliance with relevant laws and institutional guidelines and that the appropriate institutional committees has approved them. And the mice were not euthanised either during or after this procedure.

Peng Sun: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mingzhao Shang: Resources, Formal analysis. Ruyan Xie: Software, Formal analysis. Yu Gao: Resources, Formal analysis. Miaomiao Tian: Visualization, Validation. Qijun Dai: Visualization, Validation. Fang Zhang: Visualization, Validation. Fang Chai: Visualization, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22171060), Purple Mountain Talent Jiangning Baijiahu project-major special project of linkedin project (2022D027).
